section: Reading Comprehension (Factual Passages, Narrative Passages, Literary Passages, Vocabulary in Context, Inference-Based Questions, Tone and Theme Identification, Fact vs Opinion, Author's Intent)

questions.passage: By late afternoon, the old public library had become quieter than usual. Rain pressed against the windows in soft sheets, and the readers inside seemed to move more slowly, as though the weather had asked them to listen. Near the history section, a retired teacher explained maps to two schoolchildren, not by pointing out facts alone but by linking each date to a human story. At the central desk, the librarian repaired torn covers with careful patience, saying that a book becomes valuable not only when it is new but when it has been kept alive by use. A few students had come only to charge their phones, yet most stayed longer than planned, drawn into the calm rhythm of pages turning. The room did not feel grand; it felt useful, and in that usefulness it seemed to offer a kind of quiet shelter that the rain outside could not disturb.
questions.question: Which statement best captures the central idea of the passage?
options: The library is portrayed as a quiet, useful shelter that brings together people and stories.
The visitors are likely to value the library more for its atmosphere and human connection than for luxury.
The passage mainly criticizes rainy library as inefficient and unnecessary.
The passage focuses on a dramatic conflict between two opposing characters.
questions.explanation:
The option matches the overall point of the passage.
The passage uses this idea to develop rainy library.
The other options are either narrower, unsupported, or too extreme for the text.

questions.passage: The old bus depot had none of the polish of a modern terminal, but it held the city's memory in a way glass buildings could not. Conductors called out routes with a confidence shaped by habit, while porters balanced sacks and suitcases as if each were a small test of balance and patience. A tea seller worked beside the wall, offering cups so hot that customers first held them with both hands before taking a sip. Every departure seemed hurried, yet every delay created a pause in which strangers exchanged advice, gossip, or the simple warning to keep an eye on one's bag. Even the cracked notice board, crowded with faded timetables, suggested that movement here was not only about travel but about routine, bargaining, waiting, and the daily discipline of leaving on time.

questions.passage: At the coastal market, the day began before sunrise, when fishermen unloaded baskets that still smelled of salt and seaweed. Vendors arranged their stalls with quick hands, placing the brightest fruit beside the freshest catch so that color might persuade where argument could not. Tourists paused to take photographs, but local buyers moved with purpose, knowing exactly which stall sold the best prawns and which one measured spices too generously. By noon, the noise rose into a chorus of bargaining voices, motorcycle horns, and gulls that circled overhead as if they too were waiting for a bargain. Though the market looked chaotic to an outsider, it worked through invisible habits: trust between buyer and seller, speed in weighing, and a shared understanding that freshness can be judged by sight, smell, and price.

The passage mainly criticizes coastal market as inefficient and unnecessary.

questions.explanation:

The passage uses this idea to develop coastal market.

questions.passage: At rush hour, the metro platform seemed to compress every kind of human schedule into one narrow strip of space. Office workers stared at their screens, students revised formulas against the pole, and an elderly woman counted the stops aloud under her breath so she would not miss her station. The train arrived with the certainty of repetition, doors opened, and the crowd changed shape in an instant: some stepped out with relief, others pushed in with the expression of people who had already spent their patience for the day. No one spoke much, yet the platform was not silent; announcements, footsteps, and the metallic closing of doors formed a rhythm that kept everyone moving. The scene suggested that modern city life is not always dramatic; often it is simply a matter of waiting, adjusting, and learning how to share a brief piece of space.

questions.passage: The repair shop sat between a pharmacy and a tailor's stall, but it attracted a different sort of loyalty. People came there carrying clocks, radios, irons, and sometimes the kind of broken things they had already decided to throw away. The owner, a thin man with grease on his fingertips, never promised miracles. He only said that many objects fail because one small part is ignored. questions.passage: During rehearsal, the theatre looked less like a glamorous stage and more like a workshop of unfinished ideas. questions.passage: The tea stall at the crossing had only four stools and a roof of tin sheets, yet it drew customers from all directions. Truck drivers, students, shopkeepers, and municipal workers stood shoulder to shoulder waiting for tea that was strong enough to restart a tired conversation. questions.passage: When the bridge construction began, the river below continued its own course with complete indifference. Engineers measured soil, workers tied steel rods, and supervisors checked drawings that looked neat on paper but changed in the dust and heat of the site. Villagers watched from a distance, hoping the bridge would shorten the trip to the market and reduce the annual inconvenience of the flood season. The project moved slowly because a bridge is not built only from materials; it is also built from permissions, weather forecasts, inspections, and the discipline to repeat the same calculation until it is safe. By the time the first span was lifted, people understood that infrastructure is often admired after completion, though its real meaning lies in the patient effort that makes crossing possible.

questions.passage: When the bridge construction began, the river below continued its own course with complete indifference. Engineers measured soil, workers tied steel rods, and supervisors checked drawings that looked neat on paper but changed in the dust and heat of the site. Villagers watched from a distance, hoping the bridge would shorten the trip to the market and reduce the annual inconvenience of the flood season. The project moved slowly because a bridge is not built only from materials; it is also built from permissions, weather forecasts, inspections, and the discipline to repeat the same calculation until it is safe. By the time the first span was lifted, people understood that infrastructure is often admired after completion, though its real meaning lies in the patient effort that makes crossing possible.

questions.passage: The waiting room outside the ward was filled with folded newspapers, half-finished prayers, and the small sounds people make when they are trying not to show worry. A nurse moved through the chairs with efficient kindness, answering questions without pretending that uncertainty could be removed by politeness alone. One man kept checking the clock, though time in that room seemed to behave differently; it stretched around every silence and shrank whenever the doctor opened the door. The room did not erase fear, but it gathered strangers into a single, patient community whose members understood that hope and anxiety often arrive together.

questions.passage: At the relief camp, blankets were stacked beside sacks of rice, and volunteers moved between the tents with lists that changed every hour. Families arrived carrying a little clothing, a utensil, and the exhausted expression of people who had left their homes in a hurry. The camp manager repeated the same instructions many times, but repetition was necessary because each visitor came with a different worry: medicine, missing papers, school books, or news of a neighbour. Despite the hardship, the camp ran on discipline as much as on sympathy. What stood out most was not only the scale of loss but the speed with which strangers became collaborators, checking names, sharing food, and trying to restore some order to lives made chaotic by water.

questions.passage: The orchard looked ordinary from the road, but in harvest season it became a place where the year could be measured by hand. Workers climbed ladders to pick apples that had reached their proper colour, while others sorted the fruit into crates according to size, bruise, and sweetness. The owner compared the season to a long sentence: one beginning in spring, growing slowly through months of pruning and watering, and ending only when the crates were sealed for transport. Children who visited the orchard for the first time assumed the work was simple because it seemed pleasant; adults knew that the sweetness of fruit depends on discipline long before the first bite. The orchard therefore offered both beauty and evidence that abundance usually has a hidden history.

The passage mainly criticizes orchard harvest as inefficient and unnecessary.
The passage focuses on a dramatic conflict between two opposing characters.
questions.explanation:
The option matches the overall point of the passage.
The passage uses this idea to develop orchard harvest.
The other options are either narrower, unsupported, or too extreme for the text.

The owner compared the season to a long sentence: one beginning in spring, growing slowly through months of pruning and watering, and ending only when the crates were sealed for transport. Children who visited the orchard for the first time assumed the work was simple because it seemed pleasant; adults knew that the sweetness of fruit depends on discipline long before the first bite. The orchard therefore offered both beauty and evidence that abundance usually has a hidden history.
questions.question: What can be reasonably inferred from the passage?
options: Children may underestimate the work because the orchard looks pleasant.
The visitors completely reject orchard harvest because it has no social value.
The passage proves that the place mentioned is expensive to maintain.
The passage suggests that speed matters more than observation in every situation.
questions.explanation:
The inference follows from repeated details rather than a single sentence.
The passage uses this idea to develop orchard harvest.
The other options are either narrower, unsupported, or too extreme for the text.

questions.passage: In winter fog, the lane lost its familiar distances. The house at the bend, the banyan tree, and even the water tap near the wall seemed to step backward into grey uncertainty. Morning walkers slowed down, not because the route was longer but because the world had become partly hidden. A cyclist rang his bell more often than necessary, as though sound could replace sight. Yet the fog had its own discipline: it forced people to pay attention to what was near, to curb speed, and to accept that clarity is not always available on demand. By the time sunlight returned, the lane looked unchanged, but those who had crossed it knew it differently.

options: Fog changes a familiar lane by reducing visibility and slowing movement.

The passage mainly criticizes winter fog as inefficient and unnecessary.
The passage focuses on a dramatic conflict between two opposing characters.
questions.explanation:

The passage uses this idea to develop winter fog.

questions.passage: The chess club met in a classroom that smelled faintly of chalk and old wood. New members often believed that chess was a game of dramatic attacks, but the senior players preferred to explain the value of small advantages, patient positioning, and the power of refusing a flashy move. The coach rarely praised a quick victory without first asking whether the winner had actually understood the position. When a beginner lost in ten moves, the club did not mock him; instead, they replayed the game and pointed out the moment where an ordinary decision had become costly. The club's seriousness did not make it dull; rather, it made every move feel meaningful, as though thought itself were the real contest.

questions.passage: Inside the overnight train compartment, the passengers created privacy from almost nothing. A curtain became a wall, a folded blanket became a pillow, and a bag under the berth became a kind of negotiable territory. Strangers who had entered with careful politeness gradually exchanged snacks, destination stories, and warnings about which station tea tasted best. The child across the aisle fell asleep first, and the mother watched him with the relief that comes when responsibility briefly loosens its grip. The supervisor explained that waste is not a single category but a collection of decisions made too late. That sentence unsettled the visitors more than any statistics could, because it suggested that throwing away is often easier than thinking ahead. Still, the unit offered a practical kind of optimism: what is sorted carefully can re-enter the world in a new form, reducing the burden placed on land and water.
questions.question: Which statement best captures the central idea of the passage?
options: The recycling unit shows how careful sorting gives discarded things a second life.
The supervisor wants visitors to see waste as a result of poor planning.
The passage mainly criticizes recycling unit as inefficient and unnecessary.
The passage focuses on a dramatic conflict between two opposing characters.
questions.explanation:
The option matches the overall point of the passage.
The passage uses this idea to develop recycling unit.
The other options are either narrower, unsupported, or too extreme for the text.

questions.passage: The science fair rewarded curiosity more than polish. Some projects were elegant, others were patched together from cardboard, wires, and ambitious handwriting, yet the questions behind them were often sharper than the displays. One student demonstrated how shade affected soil temperature; another tested whether a homemade filter could improve muddy water. Teachers walked from table to table asking what had surprised the students, because surprise often matters more than correct answers in the first attempt. The fair suggested that science in school is not merely a set of facts to memorize; it is a way of noticing patterns, testing guesses, and learning to respect evidence even when it complicates expectation.

questions.passage: The public park was busiest not at noon but just after sunrise, when walkers claimed the paths before the heat arrived. A group of pensioners used the benches as a meeting place, discussing weather, medicines, cricket scores, and the quality of the new municipal lamps. Children on the swings created a different soundtrack altogether, while joggers moved through the same space as though the park belonged to their breathing. The grass was worn thin in some places, yet the park continued to perform its social work: offering shade, exercise, conversation, and an excuse to leave the house without a destination. Its value lay less in design than in the repeated way different ages used it together.
